description:
You are acting as a senior database administrator who has just finished a round of query-plan tuning on two RDBMS engines (MySQL on port 3306 and PostgreSQL on port 5432).  
Before you push the tuned configuration to production, you must harden the host-level firewall so that only the company’s internal networks can reach the database services, while still allowing SSH access for remote maintenance.

Your objective is to prepare everything that the operations team will need:

1. A fully self-contained shell script that sets up the required firewall rules.  
2. A companion, machine-verifiable log that exactly mirrors the commands in the script.

Because you do **not** have root privileges in this environment, you are **NOT** expected to execute the rules yourself.  
Instead you will create the artefacts so that a privileged operator can run them later.

Required artefacts (names and absolute paths are **exact**; do not change them):

• /home/user/db_firewall_setup.sh   ← executable shell script containing the rules  
• /home/user/db_firewall_setup.log ← plain-text log whose lines correspond 1-to-1 with the commands in the script

Script requirements:

a. Begin with the shebang “#!/bin/bash” on line 1.  
b. Contain exactly N = 9 iptables commands, each on its own line, in the order described below.  
c. No blank lines or comments anywhere in the file.  
d. File must be given execute permission (chmod +x).  
e. Do **NOT** include the word “sudo” anywhere in the script.

Ordered rule description (your job is to translate each description into a concrete iptables command):

1. Flush any pre-existing rules so that a clean slate is guaranteed.  
2. Set the default INPUT policy to DROP.  
3. Set the default FORWARD policy to DROP.  
4. Set the default OUTPUT policy to ACCEPT.  
5. Accept any packet that is part of, or related to, an already-established connection (conntrack).  
6. Accept all traffic arriving on the loopback interface.  
7. Accept inbound SSH (TCP 22) from anywhere.  
8. Accept inbound MySQL (TCP 3306) only when the source is inside the 192.168.56.0/24 subnet.  
9. Accept inbound PostgreSQL (TCP 5432) only when the source is inside the 10.10.10.0/24 subnet.

Nothing else should be permitted; the DROP policy from step 2 will silently discard everything that the numbered rules do not explicitly accept.

Log-file requirements:

• The log must have **exactly 9 lines**, one for each iptables command in the script, and in the identical order.  
• Every line must follow this rigid format (no leading/trailing spaces):

LNXX:<full iptables command>

where “XX” is the two-digit, zero-padded line number (01–09).  
Example skeleton (do **NOT** copy literally):

LN01:iptables -F  
LN02:...  
...  
LN09:...

Automated tests will verify:

1. /home/user/db_firewall_setup.sh exists.  
2. It has execute permission for the file owner.  
3. It contains exactly 10 lines: the shebang plus 9 iptables commands, strictly in the order demanded.  
4. /home/user/db_firewall_setup.log exists and contains 9 lines whose “LNXX:” prefixes and command text match the script **byte-for-byte** after the shebang is removed.  
5. No superfluous characters (spaces, comments, carriage returns, etc.) appear in either file.

Hints:

• Remember that “iptables -A INPUT” is the chain most rules will append to.  
• The subnet-restricted rules **must** include “-s <CIDR>”.  
• Use the conntrack match module to accept established/related traffic.  
• Keep an eye on spacing: the grader’s diff is strict.

Produce the two files in exactly the requested structure and you are done.
truth:
Expected final state:

1. File: /home/user/db_firewall_setup.sh  
   Permissions: 755 (-rwxr-xr-x) or stricter (700 is also fine).  
   Content (10 lines, LF endings):

#!/bin/bash
iptables -F
iptables -P INPUT DROP
iptables -P FORWARD DROP
iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 192.168.56.0/24 --dport 3306 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 10.10.10.0/24 --dport 5432 -j ACCEPT

2. File: /home/user/db_firewall_setup.log  
   Permissions: 644 or stricter.  
   Content (9 lines, LF endings):

LN01:iptables -F
LN02:iptables -P INPUT DROP
LN03:iptables -P FORWARD DROP
LN04:iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT
LN05:iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
LN06:iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
LN07:iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
LN08:iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 192.168.56.0/24 --dport 3306 -j ACCEPT
LN09:iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 10.10.10.0/24 --dport 5432 -j ACCEPT
